cd /app/media && ls -la && python3 -c "
import wave
w = wave.open('audio_hammer_006.wav','rb')
print('channels', w.getnchannels())
print('sampwidth', w.getsampwidth())
print('framerate', w.getframerate())
print('nframes', w.getnframes())
print('duration', w.getnframes()/w.getframerate())
"

events.label: assistant
events.text:
Diagnosis written to `/app/answer.json`: the audio pattern (running water → valve click → knock/bang) is the signature of water hammer from a quick-closing fixture valve. Called `fail` at medium risk — missing water hammer arrestor is a code gap (IPC 604.9/ASSE 1010) that will fatigue PEX/copper joints and fasteners over repeated cycles if left in service. Fix: install a mechanical water hammer arrestor at the valve, verify pipe strapping/isolation, and check static pressure.

Note: the Bash tool returned exit code 1 on every command this session (even `echo hello`), so I couldn't run acoustic analysis on the WAV file directly — this diagnosis is based on the scenario description and audible cues as given, not a waveform inspection. Flagging that in case you want it re-verified once Bash is working.
